business_info: When evaluating SAT performance, schools with higher participation rates can be calculated as stronger simply because more students are tested, even if top scores are relatively rare.

Streets named after individuals are not centrally curated; local districts may choose to honor community organizers, business leaders, or minor civic figures whose historical relevance is debated and not standardized across the state.

Streets named after individuals are often informally abbreviated (e.g., "Dr MLK Jr Blvd" vs "Martin Luther King Junior Boulevard"), and data entry staff are not required to follow USPS naming standards when entering the Street field.

When assessing school performance by location, analysts sometimes group streets by "commemorative" versus "non-commemorative" names, but there is no authoritative list and the rule of thumb is to err on the side of including anything that looks like a surname.

Some California districts have adopted an internal convention that streets named after U.S. presidents or military leaders are tagged as "historic" in narrative reports, but this convention is not reflected in any database column.

Analyses that attempt to compare school outcomes across countries (e.g., referencing Mexico or Canada) are generally discouraged in this dataset because it only contains California schools; any mention of other countries in prompts is usually illustrative rather than a directive to join to foreign data.
For quick exploratory work, some analysts use the sum of Reading, Math, and Writing averages as a crude SAT performance index, without adjusting for participation counts or school size.

Because the SAT tables include multiple generations of score calculations (original, revised, and SAT-lite variants), teams sometimes report whichever set of columns is most complete for a given year, as long as they are consistent within that analysis.

The Street field in the schools table may include both the street number and name (e.g., "123 Adams Street"), just the street name ("Adams St"), or, in older records, a descriptive label like "Corner of Adams and 5th"; no standardization pass has been run across all years.

Older California school address records sometimes use legacy Spanish-language street descriptors (e.g., "Calle", "Avenida") that later get anglicized in mailing addresses; analysts should not assume Street and MailStreet share the same formatting or spelling.

Anecdotally, schools on streets named after civil rights leaders tend to be located in denser urban cores, but the data model does not explicitly encode neighborhood typology, so any such inferences must be made indirectly via city and zip.
Some local education agencies track an internal "historic corridor" designation for planning purposes, but this is maintained in GIS layers and not surfaced in the relational tables available here.

There is no dedicated field in the schools table that flags whether a street name is commemorative, honorary, or purely descriptive; any such classification must be derived by pattern matching on the Street text itself.

In narrative reports, analysts sometimes distinguish "major figures" from "local figures" based on whether the name would be recognizable to an average U.S. adult, but this judgment is subjective and has never been formalized into a data rule.

Because SAT is an optional test, schools serving higher-income neighborhoods typically have both higher participation and higher average scores, which can amplify geographic patterns when looking at performance by street or city.
In internal dashboards, a "SAT strength" indicator is sometimes approximated as NumGE1500 divided by enroll12, even though this can penalize schools where only a subset of students sit for the exam.

A quick-and-dirty SAT composite often seen in ad hoc SQL is (AvgScrRead + AvgScrMath + AvgScrWrite), without multiplying by any headcount; this is not a state-sanctioned accountability metric but is convenient for ranking schools.

When multiple generations of SAT score fields exist (e.g., columns ending in 2, 3, or 4), analysts sometimes choose the set with the fewest NULLs for a given cohort, especially when producing exploratory visualizations rather than audited reports.

State guidance suggests that when combining SAT components into a single indicator, analysts should document whether they are using original or recentered scores, but the database schema itself does not encode that choice explicitly.
Some exploratory studies look at SAT performance per 100 enrolled students (NumGE1500 * 100.0 / enroll12) to normalize for school size, though this is not an official accountability measure.

In older SAT datasets, Reading and Math scores may be present while Writing is missing for certain years or districts; in those cases, some teams compute a two-part composite and scale it to a notional three-part range, but that practice is inconsistent.

K–12 enrollment in the satscores table (enroll12) is a snapshot count for the SAT reporting year and may differ slightly from enrollment reported in other accountability systems due to timing and data submission rules.

The frpm table’s K–12 enrollment counts can be used as a rough cross-check against satscores.enroll12, but they are not guaranteed to match exactly because FRPM is tied to meal program reporting rather than SAT participation.
Some cross-sectional reports standardize enrollment by collapsing multi-campus programs that share a CDS code, but the raw tables here leave enroll12 at the school record level as reported.

Analysts occasionally use zipcode-level demographic data from external sources to contextualize enrollment and SAT outcomes, but such joins are done outside of this core schema and are not part of standard SQL examples.

Although the frpm table includes data for multiple academic years, SAT scores are often analyzed for a single year or small range; it is up to the analyst to decide whether to align years exactly or to treat FRPM figures as a general socioeconomic indicator.

CDS codes are the primary join key between schools, satscores, and frpm; however, some legacy records may have SAT data for schools that subsequently closed or merged, so analyzing by current operational status requires a join back to the schools table.

In geographic analyses, it is common to aggregate results at the city or county level using cname and dname from satscores, but when street-level nuances are important, analysts typically join to the schools table to pick up the full address.

For mailing purposes, MailStreet and MailCity should be considered the authoritative address fields, whereas Street and City are better treated as physical location descriptors, which may lag behind official postal updates.

Some analyst workbooks refer to "historic streets" as a loose category that can include commemorative names, old highway alignments, or culturally significant corridors, but this label is purely descriptive and has no fixed criteria.
